hook.text: 'If it rains, then I take an umbrella' means the same as 'No rain, or I take an umbrella.'

buildup.text: Two statements are logically equivalent when they have identical truth tables for every input.

discovery.text: De Morgan's Laws show how NOT distributes over AND and OR—swapping one for the other.

twist.text: Many arguments fail because people confuse a statement with its converse—a classic equivalence mistake.

climax.text: Logical equivalences simplify complex expressions in both math proofs and computer circuit design.

punchline.text: Different words, same logic. That's equivalence.

quiz.question: When are two logical statements equivalent?
options: When they have identical truth values for every input
When they use the same words
When they reach the same conclusion once
